title: Grobase offer comparison — vs Supabase and MongoDB Atlas
intro: Grobase ships five honest tiers on one codebase — every number is measured against packages.json and the bench/scale artifacts. Here we line them up, dollar-for-dollar and feature-for-feature, against Supabase's published pricing and MongoDB Atlas (whose App Services BaaS layer reached end-of-life on 2025-09-30).

grobaseTiers.label: Pro
grobaseTiers.engineList: postgresql, sqlite, mysql, mariadb, mongodb, redis, cockroachdb

comparisons.verdict: Supabase is an excellent, polished single-Postgres BaaS with a beautiful Studio, mature auth, and a big community — if you only ever need PostgreSQL and you want a hosted dashboard out of the box, pick them. Where Grobase wins: nine engines instead of one, four selectable isolation models, and per-request RLS that lets a single shared pool carry 24,887 measured tenants at 2.9 MiB of data-plane RSS — so our per-tenant cost stays flat where a Supabase project is one Postgres database per project. Their Team tier ($599/mo) buys SOC 2 + ISO 27001 and dashboard SSO that we do not yet certify; our growth/Pro tier covers the same engines-plus-realtime-plus-analytics surface at $79. Choose Supabase if you want a managed dashboard + formal compliance today; choose Grobase if you want engine choice, self-host parity, and flat-cost multi-tenancy you can measure.

priceLadder.label: Free / entry
priceLadder.note: Grobase Nano is a 5 MB / 2.1 MiB binary you self-host with zero standing cost; Supabase Free gives 2 projects, 500 MB DB, 50k MAU, and pauses a project after ~1 week of inactivity.

priceLadder.label: Single product
priceLadder.note: Comparable price; Supabase Pro includes $10/mo compute credit (Small/Medium compute bills above it), 8 GB DB, 100k MAU. Grobase Essential adds a second engine + aggregate at 2 mounts.

priceLadder.label: Growth / team
priceLadder.note: Supabase Team ($599/mo) is priced for SOC 2 + ISO 27001 + 14-day backups + dashboard SSO. Grobase Pro ($79) delivers 7 engines, transactions, realtime + analytics; we do not yet sell formal compliance — that gap is honest.

features.grobase: 9 (pg, mysql, mariadb, mongo, mssql, cockroach, redis, sqlite, http)
features.rival: PostgreSQL only

features.grobase: 24,887 tenants @ 2.9 MiB data-plane RSS, 0 standing pools (per-request RLS)
features.rival: One project = one Postgres DB; tenancy only via RLS inside a single project

features.grobase: 4 selectable (shared_rls, schema, db, instance)
features.rival: RLS within a single project

features.grobase: WS/SSE subscribe + publish (Rust event bus), Pro/Max add-on
features.rival: 200 concurrent / 2M mess-mo on Free; Postgres CDC realtime

features.grobase: S3/MinIO (or any S3-compatible: R2/Backblaze/Wasabi/AWS) buckets, list, object, presigned sign + on-the-fly image transforms (resize/webp/jpeg/png/avif, gate m95) + per-bucket ABAC + per-request owner-scoping; self-host = NO per-GB / egress / total-storage cap (you pay only your own bucket)
features.rival: 1 GB Free / 100 GB Pro (hard quota), mature managed CDN + image transforms

features.grobase: Deno runtime: deploy/list/invoke + DB-event triggers (cross-tenant-isolated) + per-fn secrets (AES-GCM) + warm pool + per-invoke mem-cap + live cron + baas CLI; self-host = NO invocation cap, data never leaves your infra
features.rival: Edge Functions, 500k invocations/mo Free (billing cap), globally distributed (Deno Deploy edge), mature managed runtime

features.grobase: Same engine as Supabase (gotrue v2.188.1 vendored) + passkeys/WebAuthn (m107), per-tenant OIDC SSO (m110), SCIM 2.0 (m111), OAuth2/OIDC+PKCE+TOTP+recovery (m41/m42); self-host = no MAU cap
features.rival: Mature GoTrue-based auth, many OAuth providers, MFA, 50k-100k MAU — but SSO/SCIM are paid Team/Enterprise only, no first-class passkeys; SAML 2.0 + Studio auth UI win
features.source: Dockerfile FROM supabase/gotrue:v2.188.1; gates m107/m110/m111/m41/m42; supabase.com/pricing (SSO/SCIM = paid)

features.grobase: Per-TENANT logical backup/restore (atomic Go-native COPY, restore one tenant in isolation, gate m87) + PITR restore-to-timestamp (WAL archive + recovery_target_time, tier retention nano1d…max90d, gate m99) + whole-cluster drill (m47); self-host = backups in your own bucket, no storage/egress markup
features.rival: Daily backups + 7-day PITR (Pro), 14-day retention (Team) — turnkey managed SLA, but whole-project only (no per-tenant restore)

features.grobase: pg_graphql (same extension as Supabase), RLS-isolation gate-proven (m59, two-tenant + anon-denied); self-host = no per-query/egress metering; opt-in glibc edition (docker-compose.graphql.yml)
features.rival: pg_graphql auto-API, default-on + hosted GraphiQL Studio explorer

features.grobase: First-class op=fulltext_search (ranked to_tsvector @@ websearch_to_tsquery, multi-column, language-aware, owner-scoped — gate m101) + op=vector_search (pgvector kNN cosine/l2/ip — gate m102); engine-agnostic, capability-gated
features.rival: pgvector + tsvector (FTS is a single-column filter operator; vector needs a hand-written SQL RPC)

features.grobase: binocle-one admin UI at /_/ ; tenant self-serve API
features.rival: Full hosted Studio (table editor, SQL editor, logs)

features.grobase: TS/JS, Python, Kotlin, Swift, Dart
features.rival: JS/TS, Flutter/Dart, Swift, Python, C#, Kotlin (community)

features.grobase: Same single codebase nano→max; OSS self-host is the default, no rewrite
features.rival: Self-host exists but lags hosted; many features cloud-first

features.grobase: In-stack OWASP-CRS WAF as the sole public listener (SQLi/XSS/traversal → 403 with CRS rule-IDs, benign passes — gate m140) + per-plane network segmentation (public edge ↛ data/control plane REFUSED, gate m140) + per-tenant IP allowlist (m106) + a copy-paste Cloudflare front-door recipe. Supabase OSS self-host ships neither an in-stack WAF nor plane isolation.
features.rival: Network restrictions / IP allow-list (managed); fronted by their own Cloudflare; no in-stack WAF in the OSS self-host stack

features.grobase: Buyer-recomputable, cryptographically tamper-evident hash-chained audit (m104) + continuous SOC2-lite evidence collector that reflects reality (m108) + GDPR hard-erase/export (m105/m109), unified into an ASVS 4.0 + SOC 2 TSC + GDPR control matrix, every row evidence-backed (gate m141). All self-host + independently re-verifiable. Honest caveat: no external SOC2/ISO/HIPAA attestation yet (an auditor + business milestone, not a code gap).
features.rival: External-attested SOC 2 Type II + ISO/IEC 27001:2022 + HIPAA BAA as a hosted product — wins on the paper cert; controls are not buyer-recomputable

features.grobase: Flat: 24,887 tenants share one pool @ 2.9 MiB; rps = measured_capacity × fair_share × 0.5
features.rival: Per-project Postgres + compute add-ons (Small ~$15, Medium ~$60 above the $10 credit)

comparisons.verdict: MongoDB Atlas is a first-class managed MongoDB — Atlas Search, vector search, Charts, and Stream Processing are genuinely strong, and if your workload is document-native and you want the best managed MongoDB operations, pick them. But the honest, decisive point for an app backend: Atlas App Services — the entire BaaS layer — reached end-of-life on 2025-09-30. The Data API, GraphQL API, Device Sync + Realm SDKs, App Services Functions, hosted Authentication/user management, Static Hosting, and Custom HTTPS Endpoints are all shut down; only Database Triggers survive. So in 2026 Atlas is a managed DATABASE, not a BaaS — the Firebase-style backend it once offered is gone. Grobase gives you that retired surface back (CRUD/aggregate API, GraphQL, auth/MFA, functions) on nine engines including MongoDB itself, self-hostable, on one codebase. Choose Atlas if you want premier managed MongoDB + Atlas Search/Charts; choose Grobase if you need the application backend Atlas no longer ships.

priceLadder.label: Free / entry
priceLadder.note: Atlas M0 is 512 MB shared storage, ~100 ops/sec — a database only, no app backend. Grobase Nano is a self-hosted 2.1 MiB binary with a full CRUD + introspect API.

priceLadder.label: Single product
priceLadder.note: Atlas Flex (GA Feb 2025, base $8 usage-capped at $30) is a managed DB scaling 100→500 ops/sec, 5 GB — still no BaaS layer. Grobase Essential is a real backend API with aggregate at $25-$39.

priceLadder.label: Growth / team
priceLadder.note: Atlas M30 (~$400/mo) is dedicated DB compute; you still build and host the application backend yourself since App Services is EOL. Grobase Pro ($79) ships the backend (7 engines incl. MongoDB, transactions, realtime, analytics).
